title: Bhagavadgītā 4
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
tradition: Hindu (Vedanta; theistic synthesis of Sāṃkhya, Yoga and Bhakti)
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 4, Jñāna-Karma-Saṃnyāsa Yoga ('Yoga of Knowledge and the Renunciation of Action'). Krishna declares that he repeatedly takes birth (avatāra) in every age to restore dharma, and explains how action performed without attachment, as sacrifice and offered up in knowledge, does not bind. The chapter introduces the parampara (succession) by which this ancient teaching is transmitted and exalts wisdom (jñāna) as the fire that consumes all karma.
history: The Bhagavadgītā is part of the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata (chs. 23-40), traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa but in fact an anonymous, possibly composite, insertion into the epic. It became the most commented-upon Hindu text, with classic bhāṣyas by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja and Madhva, and was canonized as one of the three foundational texts (prasthānatrayī) of Vedānta.

title: Bhagavadgītā 5
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
tradition: Hindu (Vedanta; synthesis of Sāṃkhya, Yoga and Bhakti)
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 5, Karma-Saṃnyāsa Yoga ('Yoga of Renunciation of Action'). Arjuna asks whether renunciation of action or the disciplined performance of action is better; Krishna answers that both lead to liberation but that karma-yoga (action without attachment) is superior. The truly wise see that the self acts not at all, attributing action to the senses and gunas, and rest in the equanimity of Brahman.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. The text harmonizes the apparent tension between renunciation and action that animates much of the epic. Extensively commented upon by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Madhva and later teachers.

title: Bhagavadgītā 6
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 6, Dhyāna Yoga or Ātmasaṃyama Yoga ('Yoga of Meditation / Self-Restraint'). Krishna sets out the practice of meditation: posture, moderation in food and sleep, and the steadying of the mind until it rests motionless 'like a lamp in a windless place.' He reassures Arjuna that the yogi who falls short does not perish but is reborn in favorable circumstances to continue, and praises the devotee united with him as the highest yogi.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This chapter is a key locus classicus for classical contemplative practice and was much used by later yoga and Vedānta commentators including Śaṅkara and Rāmānuja.

title: Bhagavadgītā 7
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 7, Jñāna-Vijñāna Yoga ('Yoga of Knowledge and Realization'). Krishna describes his twofold nature: the lower (eightfold material prakṛti) and the higher (the life-principle that sustains the cosmos). He explains how the gunas and divine māyā veil him from beings, distinguishes four kinds of devotees who turn to him, and declares himself the ultimate ground behind all forms of worship.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This chapter opens the middle section (chs. 7-12) emphasizing divine nature and devotion, central to the theistic Vedānta of Rāmānuja and Madhva.

title: Bhagavadgītā 8
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 8, Akṣara-Brahma Yoga ('Yoga of the Imperishable Brahman'). Krishna answers Arjuna's questions about Brahman, the self, action, and the divine, and teaches that whatever state of being one remembers at the moment of death determines one's next condition; hence one should remember him constantly. The chapter also expounds the cosmic cycles (the day and night of Brahmā) and the bright and dark paths by which the dying depart.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. Its teaching on remembrance at death (anta-kāla-smaraṇa) became foundational for Hindu devotional eschatology and was developed by Vaiṣṇava commentators.

title: Bhagavadgītā 9
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 9, Rāja-Vidyā Rāja-Guhya Yoga ('Yoga of the Royal Knowledge and Royal Secret'). Krishna reveals the most confidential knowledge: that all beings abide in him while he remains unattached, pervading the cosmos unseen. He declares that even a simple offering of a leaf, flower, fruit or water given with devotion is accepted, and that even the lowliest devotee who worships him with single-minded love attains him.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This chapter is among the strongest statements of bhakti in the Gītā and was central to the devotional theologies of Rāmānuja and the Vaiṣṇava traditions.

title: Bhagavadgītā 10
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 10, Vibhūti Yoga ('Yoga of the Divine Glories'). Krishna enumerates his manifold manifestations (vibhūtis), declaring himself the supreme principle behind the most excellent of every class of being: among the Ādityas Viṣṇu, among rivers the Ganges, among mountains Meru, and so forth. He teaches that whatever is glorious, beautiful or powerful springs from a fragment of his splendor.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. The vibhūti listing furnished later Hindu theology and poetry with a vocabulary for perceiving the divine immanent in the world; it prepares the reader for the theophany of chapter 11.

title: Bhagavadgītā 11
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 11, Viśvarūpa-Darśana Yoga ('Yoga of the Vision of the Universal Form'). At Arjuna's request, Krishna grants him divine sight and reveals his cosmic form (viśvarūpa), the entire universe with its gods and worlds blazing within a single body of innumerable mouths and eyes. Terrified at seeing warriors rushing into the devouring jaws of time, Arjuna praises Krishna and begs him to resume his gentle human shape.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. The theophany is the dramatic and theological climax of the Gītā, famous for the verse 'I am become Time (kāla), destroyer of worlds,' and has been a touchstone of Hindu art, devotion, and modern reception.

title: Bhagavadgītā 12
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 12, Bhakti Yoga ('Yoga of Devotion'). Arjuna asks which is superior: worship of the personal, manifest God, or of the unmanifest Absolute. Krishna replies that both reach him, but the path of the formless is harder for embodied beings, so loving devotion to his personal form is recommended; he closes by describing the qualities of the devotee who is dear to him.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This short chapter is a central proof-text for the saguna/nirguna debate and for the primacy of bhakti, prized especially by Rāmānuja's school.

title: Bhagavadgītā 13
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 13, Kṣetra-Kṣetrajña-Vibhāga Yoga ('Yoga of the Distinction between the Field and the Knower of the Field'). Krishna distinguishes the 'field' (kṣetra, the body and material nature) from the 'knower of the field' (kṣetrajña, the conscious self), and identifies himself as the knower in all fields. He expounds prakṛti and puruṣa in Sāṃkhya terms and teaches that liberation comes from discerning the difference between the perishable field and the imperishable knower.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This chapter opens the final group (chs. 13-18), the most overtly Sāṃkhya-Vedānta in idiom, and is heavily drawn on in Vedānta epistemology and metaphysics.

title: Bhagavadgītā 14
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 14, Guṇatraya-Vibhāga Yoga ('Yoga of the Division of the Three Gunas'). Krishna analyzes the three constituents of material nature, sattva (purity), rajas (passion) and tamas (inertia), describing how each binds the embodied self and conditions rebirth. He teaches that the liberated person transcends the gunas (guṇātīta) and that such transcendence is reached through unwavering devotion to him.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. The systematic treatment of the gunas links the Gītā closely to Sāṃkhya psychology while subordinating it to bhakti.

title: Bhagavadgītā 15
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 15, Puruṣottama Yoga ('Yoga of the Supreme Person'). Krishna describes the cosmos as an inverted aśvattha (fig) tree whose roots are above and branches below, to be cut down with the axe of non-attachment. He then distinguishes the perishable (kṣara) and imperishable (akṣara) beings and declares himself the supreme person (puruṣottama) who transcends both, the source of memory, knowledge and the Vedas.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. The cosmic-tree imagery draws on Upaniṣadic precedents (Kaṭha Upaniṣad), and the puruṣottama doctrine is foundational for Vaiṣṇava theology.

title: Bhagavadgītā 16
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 16, Daivāsura-Sampad-Vibhāga Yoga ('Yoga of the Division between Divine and Demonic Endowments'). Krishna contrasts the qualities of those born to a 'divine' (daivī) destiny, fearlessness, purity, non-violence, truthfulness, with the 'demonic' (āsurī) traits of arrogance, lust, greed and hypocrisy. He warns that those given over to demonic conduct fall into ruin, and commends scripture (śāstra) as the authority for determining right action.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. This chapter offers the Gītā's clearest moral typology of human character and its appeal to scriptural authority in conduct.

title: Bhagavadgītā 17
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 17, Śraddhātraya-Vibhāga Yoga ('Yoga of the Division of the Threefold Faith'). Krishna explains that faith (śraddhā), like food, sacrifice, austerity and giving, takes three forms according to the predominant guna (sattva, rajas, tamas). He also expounds the sacred formula 'oṃ tat sat' as designations of Brahman that consecrate ritual acts.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. The chapter integrates Vedic ritual categories into the Gītā's guna-based ethics and supplies the much-cited 'oṃ tat sat' triad.

title: Bhagavadgītā 18
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
tradition: Hindu (Vedanta; synthesis of Karma, Jñāna and Bhakti)
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed); chapter of a composite work
summary: Chapter 18, Mokṣa-Saṃnyāsa Yoga ('Yoga of Liberation through Renunciation'), the longest and concluding chapter. Krishna distinguishes renunciation (saṃnyāsa) from relinquishment of the fruits of action (tyāga), reviews knowledge, action and agents according to the three gunas, and describes duties suited to one's nature (svadharma). He culminates in his supreme counsel, to abandon all dharmas and take refuge in him alone, after which Arjuna, his doubts dispelled, resolves to fight.
history: Part of the Bhagavadgītā within the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata. As the summation of the dialogue, this chapter recapitulates the whole teaching; its 'charama śloka' (18.66) is the most debated verse in Vaiṣṇava soteriology, central to Rāmānuja and the Śrīvaiṣṇava prapatti doctrine.

title: Bhagavadgītā
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
tradition: Hindu (Vedanta; synthesis of Sāṃkhya, Yoga and Bhakti)
genre: philosophical-religious poem (dialogue); didactic episode embedded in epic; foundational Vedānta text (prasthānatrayī)
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (disputed; often placed 1st c. BCE)
summary: The Bhagavadgītā ('Song of the Lord') is a 700-verse dialogue, set on the battlefield of Kurukṣetra, in which the warrior Arjuna, despondent at the prospect of killing his kinsmen, is counseled by his charioteer Krishna, who is revealed as God. Across eighteen chapters Krishna synthesizes the paths of disciplined action (karma-yoga), knowledge (jñāna-yoga) and devotion (bhakti-yoga), teaching the imperishable self, the nature of the divine, and liberation. It culminates in the vision of Krishna's cosmic form and the call to take refuge in him.
history: An anonymous, probably composite, work inserted into the Bhīṣmaparvan of the Mahābhārata and traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. It became the most influential and most commented-upon text of Hinduism, canonized with the Upaniṣads and Brahmasūtra as one of the three pillars (prasthānatrayī) of Vedānta and the subject of major bhāṣyas by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja and Madhva; in the modern era it shaped figures from Gandhi to global yoga.

title: Mahābhārata: Ādiparvan
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: epic (itihāsa); section (parvan) of the Mahābhārata
date_estimate: c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE (composite); core narrative perhaps Mauryan-Gupta period
summary: The Ādiparvan ('Book of the Beginning') is the first of the eighteen books of the Mahābhārata. It frames the epic (Vyāsa's composition, the snake sacrifice of Janamejaya, the bard Ugraśravas) and narrates the origins and youth of the Bhārata dynasty: the births of the Kuru and Pāṇḍava princes, the rivalry of the cousins, episodes such as the burning of the lac house and the contest for Draupadī, and the partition of the kingdom. It also contains famous embedded tales like Śakuntalā and the churning of the ocean.
history: Traditionally ascribed to the sage Vyāsa but in fact the product of centuries of oral and redactional growth. Its critical edition was the first volume of the Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute project, edited by V. S. Sukthankar (Adiparvan published 1933), whose prolegomena established the methodology for the whole critical edition (completed 1966).

title: Mahābhārata: Udyogaparvan
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: epic (itihāsa); section (parvan) of the Mahābhārata
date_estimate: c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE (composite)
summary: The Udyogaparvan ('Book of the Effort/Preparation') is the fifth book of the Mahābhārata. After the Pāṇḍavas' exile ends, it recounts the failed diplomacy and mobilization for war: both sides seek allies, and Krishna goes to Hastināpura as peace envoy, but Duryodhana refuses any compromise. It contains the moral counsel given to Dhṛtarāṣṭra by Vidura (the Vidura-nīti) and the teaching of Sanatsujāta, and ends with the armies marshalled for battle.
history: Part of the composite Mahābhārata traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. Its critical edition was prepared at the Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute by S. K. De. The didactic insertions (Vidura-nīti, Sanatsujātīya) made it a favored source of nīti (ethical-political) wisdom.

title: Mahābhārata: Bhīṣmaparvan
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: epic (itihāsa); section (parvan) of the Mahābhārata
date_estimate: c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE (composite)
summary: The Bhīṣmaparvan ('Book of Bhīṣma') is the sixth book of the Mahābhārata and the first of the war books. It opens the eighteen-day battle of Kurukṣetra under the generalship of the Kuru patriarch Bhīṣma, narrating the first ten days of fighting until Bhīṣma is felled by Arjuna (aided by Śikhaṇḍin) and lies on a bed of arrows. This book contains the Bhagavadgītā (chapters 23-40), Krishna's battlefield discourse to Arjuna.
history: Part of the composite Mahābhārata traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. Its critical edition was edited by S. K. Belvalkar at the Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute. The book's inclusion of the Bhagavadgītā made it by far the most influential of the parvans in religious and philosophical history.

title: Mahābhārata: Sauptikaparvan
author: attributed to Vyāsa (traditional); anonymous/composite
genre: epic (itihāsa); section (parvan) of the Mahābhārata
date_estimate: c. 4th c. BCE - 4th c. CE (composite)
summary: The Sauptikaparvan ('Book of the Sleeping/Night Attack') is the tenth and one of the shortest books of the Mahābhārata. After the war has ended in Pāṇḍava victory, the surviving Kaurava warrior Aśvatthāman, with Kṛpa and Kṛtavarman, raids the enemy camp by night and massacres the sleeping Pāṇḍava army, including Draupadī's sons. In the ensuing pursuit Aśvatthāman releases the apocalyptic brahmaśiras weapon, which Vyāsa and Krishna intervene to neutralize, and he is cursed for the atrocity.
history: Part of the composite Mahābhārata traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. Its critical edition was prepared at the Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute (volume with the Striparvan, ed. H. D. Velankar and V. G. Paranjpe). W. J. Johnson's translation 'The Sauptikaparvan: The Massacre at Night' (Oxford) gave it wide modern currency as a meditation on the ethics of revenge and total war.
